text: To beg to differ
A fair point
To push back
Common ground
To concede
To be at odds
To meet halfway
To agree to disagree
A counterargument
With all due respect
To take issue with
To play devil's advocate
With respect, I see it differently
With all due respect, I beg to differ.
Although I see your point, I still disagree.
You love museums, whereas I prefer markets.
Even though it was expensive, it was worth it.
While you plan everything, I leave room for surprises.
Honestly, I think we should skip the coast. It is a long drive for a beach.
That is a fair point, the drive is long. With all due respect, though, I beg to differ, the sunset there is extraordinary.
Let me play devil's advocate. Whereas the coast is calm, the old town has the markets you love.
I will concede that. Although I would love the markets, I do not want to be at odds with you all day. Can we meet halfway?
I think we just found our common ground. Markets in the morning, coast for sunset.
Perfect. Even though we started at odds, we never had to agree to disagree. That is a good travel partner.

Honestly, I used to dread disagreeing with people I had just met on a trip. I was so afraid of sounding rude that I would just nod and say nothing, even though I clearly thought otherwise. Then a friend taught me a simple trick. You start by conceding something true. You say, that is a fair point, or I can absolutely see where you are coming from. Only then do you gently push back with your own view. The little word in the middle does the work. Although I agree the old town is beautiful, I would rather spend my day by the sea. Whereas before I stayed silent, now I beg to differ in a warm way, and people actually enjoy the conversation more. We rarely end up enemies. Usually we find some common ground, and when we cannot, we simply agree to disagree and move on to the next glass of wine.

Here is something I learned the hard way as a tour guide. Travelers do not really want a guide who is always right. They want one who can disagree gracefully. I remember a guest who was convinced our coastal route was a waste of time. With all due respect, I told him, I take issue with that, although I completely understand why a busy person would skip it. Then I offered my counterargument. Whereas the city is crowded all summer, the coast is calm and the light at sunset is extraordinary. He still played devil's advocate for a while, pushing back just to test me. But by the end, we had met halfway. He kept his morning in the museums, while I won him over to an afternoon by the water. He later told me it was the best part of his trip. Being at odds, it turns out, is not a problem, as long as you stay kind.
